title: Word Break
code: public bool WordBreak(string s, IList<string> wordDict)

frames.caption: Base case: dp[0] = true — the empty prefix is segmentable with no words. Fill dp[1..8] left to right.
frames.note: dp[i] answers one yes/no question: can the first i characters of s be cut into dictionary words? The empty prefix needs no words, so dp[0] = true is the free base case — and it is the seed every later answer chains back to. Without it the whole table would stay false. We then fill dp[1..n] left to right, because dp[i] only ever reads earlier cells dp[j] with j < i, so each one is ready by the time we need it.

frames.caption: Compute dp[1]: can s[0..0] = "l" be segmented? Scan cut points j = 0..0.
frames.note: To decide dp[i], we ask: is there a cut point j where the prefix s[0..j-1] is already segmentable (dp[j] == true) AND the leftover chunk s[j..i-1] is a single dictionary word? We scan every j from 0 up to i-1. dp[j] being true means "the first j chars already split cleanly"; the slice s[j..i-1] is then the one new word we would append. Both halves must hold for the split to work.

frames.caption: j=0: dp[0]=true but "l" is not in the dictionary — this cut fails, try the next j.
frames.note: A cut point only helps when BOTH halves hold: dp[j] true and s[j..i-1] a dictionary word. Cut points with dp[j] false are skipped at a glance (no segmentable prefix to extend). The remaining candidates fail when their leftover slice is not a real word, so we move the cut and keep looking; dp[i] stays false until some j satisfies both conditions, and if none does, dp[i] is false.

frames.caption: No cut point worked: dp[1] = false. s[0..0] = "l" cannot be segmented.
frames.note: A cut point only helps when BOTH halves hold: dp[j] true and s[j..i-1] a dictionary word. Cut points with dp[j] false are skipped at a glance (no segmentable prefix to extend). The remaining candidates fail when their leftover slice is not a real word, so we move the cut and keep looking; dp[i] stays false until some j satisfies both conditions, and if none does, dp[i] is false.

frames.caption: j=0: dp[0]=true and "leet" is in the dictionary → dp[4] = true. Break — one split is enough.
frames.note: Both halves hold: dp[j] is true and s[j..i-1] is in the dictionary, so the first i characters split cleanly as (a valid segmentation of the first j) + (the new word). Set dp[i] = true and BREAK — one valid split is all we need to prove segmentability, so there is no reason to test the remaining cut points.

frames.caption: Answer is dp[8] = true — "leetcode" splits as "leet" + "code".
frames.note: dp[n] is the whole-string answer: can all n characters be segmented? Here dp[8] = true, witnessed by the split "leet" + "code". Each of the n cells scanned up to n earlier cells doing an O(L) substring + hash lookup, so the cost is O(n^2 * L) time and O(n) space — and a single HashSet gives O(1)-average word checks.
